title: Trauma and the Salience Network: Hypervigilance, Threat Detection, and Healing
excerpt: Explore how trauma rewires the brain's salience network, driving hypervigilance and threat detection, and learn evidence-based paths to somatic healing.
body:
Introduction: The Brain on Trauma

Many people who have experienced trauma report feeling constantly on edge, scanning their environment for danger even when there is none. This hypervigilance is not just psychological—it’s a deeply embodied, neurological state. Central to this experience is the brain’s salience network, a system that helps us detect and respond to important stimuli in our environment. When trauma occurs, this network can become overactive or dysregulated, leading to persistent states of alertness and anxiety.

Understanding the salience network’s role in trauma can help those stuck in cycles of fear and reactivity find a clear path toward healing. Grounded in the work of neuroscientists and trauma researchers like Bessel van der Kolk, Peter Levine, Stephen Porges, and Deb Dana, this article unpacks the science behind hypervigilance and offers practical insights for somatic recovery.

What Is the Salience Network?

The salience network is a complex brain system primarily responsible for identifying the most relevant internal and external stimuli and guiding attention accordingly. It acts as a switchboard, prioritizing what deserves our focus and helping us decide how to respond.

Key nodes of the salience network include the anterior insula and the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC). These regions work together to assess the emotional and physiological importance of stimuli—like a sudden loud noise or a subtle change in a person’s tone of voice—and mobilize appropriate responses, such as fight, flight, or social engagement.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory complements this understanding by explaining how the autonomic nervous system’s regulation ties into these brain networks, influencing our sense of safety and connection.

Trauma’s Impact on the Salience Network

Trauma, especially when chronic or early in life, can recalibrate the salience network’s sensitivity. Research shows that individuals with post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) and related conditions exhibit increased activity in the anterior insula and dACC, signaling heightened awareness of potential threats—even in safe environments.

Bessel van der Kolk’s seminal work The Body Keeps the Score outlines how trauma is not just stored as memories but also encoded in the body and brain’s threat detection systems, including the salience network.

This hyperactive state leads to hypervigilance—where the brain is perpetually scanning for danger, creating chronic anxiety, difficulty concentrating, and fatigue. The world feels unsafe, triggering automatic defensive responses.

Neuroimaging Evidence

  • Increased anterior insula activation: Studies using fMRI show that trauma survivors have elevated insula responses to neutral or ambiguous stimuli, interpreting them as threatening (Nicholson et al., 2020).
  • Heightened dACC activity: The dACC, involved in conflict monitoring and emotional regulation, is hyperactive in PTSD, contributing to difficulty modulating fear responses (Etkin & Wager, 2007).
  • Impaired connectivity: The salience network’s communication with the default mode and executive networks is disrupted, impairing the ability to shift attention away from perceived threats (Sripada et al., 2012).

Why Hypervigilance Feels So Exhausting

Hypervigilance is not just a mental state—it’s a physical drain. The body remains in a state of high arousal, with elevated heart rate, muscle tension, and hormone secretion (e.g., cortisol, adrenaline). This chronic stress response taxes the nervous system and immune function.

Dan Siegel and Deb Dana emphasize that when the salience network is overactive, it hijacks the prefrontal cortex’s regulatory functions, making it difficult to calm down or think clearly. The result is a vicious cycle: the brain perceives threat, triggers alertness, and suppresses the calming systems, which then fuels more threat perception.

Somatic Healing: Recalibrating the Salience Network

Healing from trauma involves more than talking through memories—it requires regulating the nervous system and restoring balance to the salience network. Here are evidence-informed somatic approaches to consider:

1. Polyvagal-Informed Practices

Deb Dana’s work on Polyvagal Theory shows how activating the ventral vagal system supports feelings of safety and social engagement, which counteracts hypervigilance.

  • Safe social connection: Engaging in compassionate relationships helps downregulate the salience network’s threat detection.
  • Breathwork: Slow, deep breathing stimulates the vagus nerve, calming arousal.

2. Mindfulness and Interoception

Mindfulness practices that cultivate nonjudgmental awareness of bodily sensations can help individuals notice hypervigilant states without being overwhelmed.

Pat Ogden’s Sensorimotor Psychotherapy emphasizes increasing interoceptive awareness—the ability to sense internal bodily signals—which aids in recognizing and regulating salience network overactivity.

3. Somatic Experiencing and Pendulation

Peter Levine’s Somatic Experiencing focuses on gently oscillating between sensations of distress and safety (“pendulation”), which helps the nervous system renegotiate traumatic energy and reduces salience network hyperactivity.

4. EMDR and Neural Integration

Francine Shapiro’s Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) facilitates integration of traumatic memories, which can help normalize salience network responses to triggers.

Practical Tips for Everyday Regulation

Understanding the salience network helps guide simple daily practices to disrupt hypervigilance cycles:

  • Grounding: Use your senses to reorient to the present moment—notice textures, sounds, smells.
  • Movement: Gentle exercise or stretching supports nervous system regulation.
  • Safe environment: Create spaces that feel physically and emotionally safe to reduce chronic alertness.
  • Limit overstimulation: Reduce exposure to unpredictable or triggering media and environments.
“Trauma recalibrates the brain’s threat detection system, making the world feel unsafe—even when it isn’t. Healing requires rebalancing that system through body-based practices that restore safety.” — The Oracle Lover

Conclusion: From Survival to Thriving

The salience network’s role in trauma offers a clear lens on why survivors experience hypervigilance and chronic threat detection. This isn’t a failure of willpower or character—it’s a brain wired to protect you from danger. The good news is that the salience network is plastic and can be recalibrated through somatic healing methods grounded in neuroscience.

By integrating polyvagal-informed practices, interoceptive awareness, somatic experiencing, and trauma-informed therapies like EMDR, you can begin to shift from surviving in a state of constant alertness to living with safety and presence. Understanding your brain’s salience network is a powerful step in reclaiming your nervous system and your life.

References

  • van der Kolk, B. (2015). The Body Keeps the Score. Viking.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice. North Atlantic Books.
  • Porges, S. W. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Neurophysiology, 113(2), 1487-1506.
  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton.
  • Ogden, P., Minton, K., & Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body. Norton.
  • Maté, G. (2019). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts. North Atlantic Books.
  • Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Nicholson, A. A., et al. (2020). The role of anterior insula in trauma-related hypervigilance. NeuroImage: Clinical, 27, 102279.
  • Etkin, A., & Wager, T. D. (2007). Functional neuroimaging of anxiety. American Journal of Psychiatry, 164(10), 1476-1488.
  • Sripada, R. K., et al. (2012). Neural dysregulation in PTSD. Biological Psychiatry, 71(4), 358-366.

faqs.q: What is the salience network and why is it important in trauma?
faqs.a: The salience network identifies important stimuli and guides attention. In trauma, it becomes overactive, leading to hypervigilance and chronic threat detection.

faqs.q: How does hypervigilance affect the body?
faqs.a: Hypervigilance keeps the nervous system in a state of high arousal, causing fatigue, anxiety, muscle tension, and hormonal imbalances that drain physical and mental energy.

faqs.q: Can somatic practices help regulate the salience network?
faqs.a: Yes. Practices like polyvagal-informed breathwork, mindfulness, somatic experiencing, and EMDR have been shown to calm the salience network and improve nervous system regulation.

faqs.q: Why do trauma survivors feel ‘stuck’ even after therapy?
faqs.a: Because trauma impacts deep brain networks and the body’s nervous system, talk therapy alone often isn’t enough. Somatic approaches that directly address bodily states are crucial for full healing.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma shapes the brain and body, offering insights critical for somatic healing.
